caseSubtitle: Closed-door hiring process and self-appointment raised accountability concerns
caseSummary: After the CAO’s retirement in 2021, the Township ran a recruitment process that lacked transparency and ended with the acting CAO appointing herself to a newly defined role. Key steps happened behind closed doors, including a meeting later deemed illegal by the Ombudsman.

caseTimeline.content: RFP responses directed to the acting CAO herself.
Closed-door Council meeting later ruled illegal by the Ombudsman.
E4m engaged to assist with recruitment.
Acting CAO signs By-law 2021-45 appointing herself as Municipal Administrator.
Organizational chart updated to reflect new structure.

caseDetails: Following the retirement of the Township’s CAO, a recruitment process was initiated that lacked independence and transparency.
The acting CAO-Clerk-Treasurer both led the process and was the recipient of submissions, creating a clear conflict of interest.
Ultimately, the process resulted in the acting CAO-Clerk-Treasurer appointing herself to the newly defined role of Municipal Administrator through By-law 2021-45.
The change from CAO-Clerk-Treasurer to Municipal Administrator has never been clearly explained to residents.
casePattern: A major staffing decision was made through a process that combined internal control, limited transparency, and closed-door decision-making.
caseDifferently: Ensure recruitment processes are fully independent from internal candidates
Require all major staffing decisions to be discussed in open session
Clearly define and publicly explain any role changes before appointments are made
Separate RFP administration from individuals who may benefit from the outcome
